nativeRange.habitat: Rocky littoral zone of Lake Malawi; found in and among boulder piles and rock rubble along the shoreline, rarely venturing over open sandy substrate
nativeRange.biotope: Rocky littoral — Lake Malawi mbuna zone

decorPreferences: large smooth boulders and rock piles creating multiple caves and territories
minimal or no planting (cichlids will uproot most plants)
open sand substrate (coarse sand or fine gravel acceptable)
multiple visual barriers to break sightlines between territorial males
aragonite or crushed coral substrate or decor to buffer pH and hardness

diet.notes: The Zebra Mbuna is primarily herbivorous, with algae (including the biofilm layer known as aufwuchs), spirulina-based flakes and pellets, blanched vegetables such as spinach and zucchini, and algae wafers forming the core of its diet. A modest protein component — small frozen foods such as daphnia, mysis, or Cyclops — is accepted and can be beneficial. High-protein foods like bloodworms should be avoided, as they can trigger Malawi Bloat, a potentially fatal digestive condition associated with excess animal protein in herbivorous mbuna. Feeding two to three small meals daily with a spirulina-enriched staple is generally recommended.

goodWith: other robust Lake Malawi mbuna of similar size (Pseudotropheus, Labidochromis, Iodotropheus)
Lake Malawi haplochromines of significantly different coloration
Synodontis catfish (Lake Tanganyika species tolerate Malawi conditions)
avoidWith: cobalt-blue mbuna (Maylandia callainos fw-fish-303) — hybridizes freely
any similarly colored or patterned mbuna (triggers inter- and intraspecific aggression)
peaceful community fish (tetras, danios, corydoras, livebearers)
small or slow fish that cannot escape harassment
mbuna from other genera that share similar blue coloration (confusion spawning risk)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Maylandia zebra. Shows typical male OB or blue coloration.
Wikimedia image labeled Pseudotropheus zebra (synonym); male blue morph in aquarium.

summary: The Zebra Mbuna (Maylandia zebra) is a Lake Malawi cichlid found along rocky shorelines and recognized across several color forms, including blue-barred, cobalt, red-top, and orange-blotched morphs. Highly territorial, it is best kept in a large, rock-filled mbuna community.
careNotes:
Maylandia zebra is the type species of its genus and one of the most extensively studied African rift-lake cichlids, collected throughout the rocky littoral zone — the shallow, boulder-strewn shoreline — of Lake Malawi. Wild populations show striking locality-based color variation: the classic blue-and-black barred form, the uniformly blue Cobalt Blue morph, the orange-red Red Top form, and the mottled orange-and-black OB (orange-blotched) pattern known as the Marmalade Cat are all recognized as the same species. These forms are frequently sold under distinct trade names, and mixing them in a breeding tank will produce hybrid offspring that cannot be distinguished from pure locality fish by appearance alone.

A minimum tank of 200 L (roughly 120 cm long) is recommended for a starter group; tanks of 300 L or more are strongly preferred when housing multiple males or a mixed-mbuna community. Mbuna are widely considered highly territorial: keeping at least one male with three to four females, or overstocking with a large mixed-mbuna community to spread aggression across multiple targets, are both commonly advised approaches. A single male confined with few females in a small tank tends to bully them relentlessly. Rockwork should be stacked high with interlocking caves to create a large number of distinct territories.

Water chemistry must be maintained carefully: pH 7.8–8.6 and moderate hardness (gH 10–20, kH 6–15) reflect the naturally hard, alkaline water of Lake Malawi. Aragonite or crushed coral substrate is a practical way to buffer both pH and hardness. A temperature of 23–28°C suits all Malawi mbuna. Weekly water changes of 30–50% are important for long-term health; nitrate accumulation above 20–25 ppm has been associated with Malawi Bloat.

The diet should remain predominantly plant-based. High-protein foods — particularly bloodworms and tubifex — are associated with Malawi Bloat, a serious and often fatal digestive condition in herbivorous mbuna. Spirulina flake, mbuna-specific pellets, and occasional frozen daphnia or mysis are appropriate staples.

Avoiding tankmate combinations that risk hybridization is important: Maylandia callainos (Cobalt Blue, [fw-fish-303]) and M. estherae (Red Zebra, [fw-fish-466]) hybridize freely with M. zebra in captivity and the resulting offspring are fertile, making pure strains difficult to maintain once mixing occurs.
breedingNotes: The Zebra Mbuna is a maternal mouthbrooder, meaning the female incubates the eggs inside her mouth throughout development rather than guarding a nest. Spawning follows the typical mbuna pattern: the male selects and defends a flat rock surface, courts the female with circling displays and fin-spreading, and the female deposits her eggs and immediately picks them up in her buccal cavity (the mouth pouch used for brooding). Fertilization occurs through the egg-dummy method — the male’s anal fin carries spots that mimic eggs, triggering the female to mouth at them and take in milt in the process. The female broods 15–40 eggs for 21–28 days at 25–26°C, releasing free-swimming fry once they have absorbed their yolk sacs. In tanks with significant predation pressure from other fish, females can be gently stripped of their eggs at day 18–21 and the eggs moved to an artificial tumbler for the remaining incubation. Brood sizes typically range from 15 to 40 fry. Released fry accept finely crushed spirulina flake or micro-pellets from the first feeding and tend to grow quickly, beginning to show adult coloration within 8–12 weeks. The brooding female should be given time to recover condition after releasing her fry, as males typically resume courtship pressure almost immediately.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Maylandia zebra
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided scientific taxonomy, native range (Lake Malawi rocky shoreline), water parameters (pH 7.5–8.8, temp 22–28°C, hardness 10–20 dH), adult size (males to 12–14 cm), tank size recommendation, diet notes (herbivore/aufwuchs), breeding method (maternal mouthbrooder, 21–28 day hold), conspecific aggression and hybridization risks with M. callainos and M. estherae.

additional.name: FishBase — Maylandia zebra
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Cichlidae, Cichliformes), adult size (males to 16 cm TL in wild, aquarium specimens typically 10–14 cm), native range (Lake Malawi endemic, Malawi/Mozambique/Tanzania), depth range (1–15 m), diet (herbivore, algae/aufwuchs), and habitat (rocky littoral). Also confirmed type-species status for Maylandia. Size range consolidated to 10–14 cm aquarium typical.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Mbuna Cichlid Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed minimum tank size (~75 gal / 280 L for species tank), alkaline hard water requirements, spirulina-heavy diet, Malawi Bloat risk with protein-heavy feeding, and overstocking strategy for aggression management. Temperature range 75–82°F (24–28°C) consistent with other sources.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Cichlids: Malawi Mbuna
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist source. Confirmed locality-form polymorphism (blue, cobalt, OB, red-top morphs all M. zebra), hybridization behavior and caution, breeding behavior detail (egg-dummy anal fin markings), and recommended sex ratio (1 male: 3–4 females). Also confirmed Pseudotropheus zebra as a prior synonym.

additional.name: Stauffer et al. 1997 / Stauffer & Konings 2006 — Maylandia type species designation
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference establishing M. zebra as the type species of Maylandia. The genus Metriaclima (Stauffer 1997) is treated as a synonym of Maylandia (Meyer & Foerster 1984) by many authorities including FishBase and SeriouslyFish; some publications still use Metriaclima. Both names appear in hobbyist literature.

fish.breedingNotes: Maternal mouthbrooder; female incubates 15–40 eggs buccally for 21–28 days. Spawning occurs readily in well-maintained tanks with appropriate rock decor. Main challenges: male aggression toward brooding female and fry predation by tankmates. Stripping females at day 18–21 improves fry yield. Fry accept micro-crushed spirulina flake from first feeding.
